content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322-5662 
Steve White 
Executive Director 
Administration 
•• 
Executive/legol 
322-5660 
32~-5901 
August 22, 1983 
California District Attorneys Association 
1130 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
RE: 
State Bar Board of Governors 
FPPC No. GC-83/262 
Advice No. 190 
Dear Mr. White: 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
You have asked for the Commission's advice as to whether you 
have any obligations under the Political Reform Act of 1974 as 
amended (Government Code Sections 81000 et seq.). 
In particular 
you have asked whether you are required to obtain a committee 
registration number from the California Secretary of State's 
office. 
The campaign registration and reporting provisions of the 
Political Reform Act apply only to "candidates" and "committees" 
as those terms are defined in the Government Code. 
Government 
Code Sections 84101 and 84200.!/ 
A candidate is a person 
running for an office in an "election." 
However, an "election" 
is defined as a primary, general, special, or recall election. 
Section 82022. 
An election by members of the State Bar for the 
Board of Governors is not an election within the meaning of the 
Act. 
As a candidate for the Board of Governors, you are not a 
"candidate" within the meaning of the Act. 
Similarly, there can 
be no "committees" as defined in the Act in connection with an 
election for membership on the Board of Governors of the State 
Bar. 
!/ All further statutory references are to the California 
Government Code. 


Mr. White 
August 22, 1983 
Page Two 
Since you are not a "candidate" as defined by the Political 
Reform Act, monies that you receive from others to assist you in 
your campaign are not considered to be campaign contributions but 
are gifts to you personally. 
Therefore, individuals providing 
monetary assistance out of their personal funds also don't have 
any obligations under the Act. 
In conclusion, your candidacy for~the State Bar Board of 
Governors does not subject you or anyone providing you with 
personal funds to any of the provisions of the Political Reform 
Act. 
If you have any further questions on this matter please 
don't hesitate to give me a call. 
2ZlJ?Zt.-
Robert D. Blasier, J 
• 
Director 
Enforcement Division 
RDB:sf

sections.question: You have asked for the Commission's advice as to whether you have any obligations under the Political Reform Act of 1974 as amended (Government Code Sections 81000 et seq.). In particular you have asked whether you are required to obtain a committee registration number from the California Secretary of State's office.
sections.conclusion: Since you are not a "candidate" as defined by the Political Reform Act, monies that you receive from others to assist you in your campaign are not considered to be campaign contributions but are gifts to you personally. Therefore, individuals providing monetary assistance out of their personal funds also don't have any obligations under the Act. In conclusion, your candidacy for the State Bar Board of Governors does not subject you or anyone providing you with personal funds to any of the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the recipient have obligations under the Political Reform Act and must they obtain a committee registration number in connection with their candidacy for the State Bar Board of Governors?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: State Bar Board of Governors elections are not elections under the Political Reform Act, so the recipient is not a "candidate" and has no obligations under the Act. Monies received are personal gifts, not campaign contributions, and neither the recipient nor donors have any Political Reform Act obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document is FPPC No. GC-83/262, Advice No. 190, dated August 22, 1983, signed by Robert D. Blasier, Director of Enforcement Division.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked for the Commission's advice as to whether you have any obligations under the Political Reform Act of 1974 as amended (Government Code Sections 81000 et seq.). In particular you have asked whether you are required to obtain a committee registration number from the California Secretary of State's office.

CONCLUSION: Since you are not a "candidate" as defined by the Political Reform Act, monies that you receive from others to assist you in your campaign are not considered to be campaign contributions but are gifts to you personally. Therefore, individuals providing monetary assistance out of their personal funds also don't have any obligations under the Act. In conclusion, your candidacy for the State Bar Board of Governors does not subject you or anyone providing you with personal funds to any of the provisions of the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that candidacy for the State Bar Board of Governors does not trigger Political Reform Act requirements because such elections are not defined as elections under the Act. Consequently, no committee registration is required and funds received are personal gifts rather than campaign contributions.
